persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: a same-name relative (e.g. a son named after him) serving in Canada, or the stint conflating two Tilleys, since 9 Tilleys appear in staff lists. But the stint records carry the full forename 'Samuel Leonard'/'Samuel L.', the exact offices the biography narrates (Lieutenant-Governor of New Brunswick to 1878, Minister of Finance from October 1878, Lieutenant-Governor again from 1885), and BOTH distinctive honours with correct chronology: C.B. alone in 1877-78, K.C.M.G. added from 1880 (awarded 1879 per the bio). No relative would replicate that honour pair and office sequence. The post-1896 listings (e.g. 1908 with no position) might suggest a different living Tilley, but they show the same K.C.M.G., C.B. pair and blank positions, consistent with stale privy-council/honours rolls listing an honoured ex-minister; the 1894/1896 entries already place him among 'Members of the Privy Council who are not now members of the Cabinet'. The counter-hypothesis fails on every specific dimension.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous 'Samuel Leonard Tilley' holding office in Canada (e.g. a son with the same full name and K.C.M.G./C.B.), or proof that the post-1900 staff-list rows refer to a different living official rather than a stale honours listing.

notes: Single bio, single stint. Multiple independent agreeing dimensions: full forename, two honours with award-date-consistent appearance, and three distinct offices matching the printed biography's sequence. Stint listings continue to 1914, well past the bio's 1885 terminal and the subject's likely death; these tail rows are position-less honours-roll entries and were treated as stale listings, not service currency.
